latest.disputed_claims: The load-bearing claim — that accumulated memory reliably PUSHES capability UP across a danger threshold — is contested. Memory research shows memory frequently degrades or destabilizes performance: retrieval accuracy tends to peak around 3–5 chunks and degrade as noise overwhelms signal (SitePoint), memory can be poisoned or grow stale (arXiv 2606.14816; DEV Community), and the observed longitudinal effect in 'temporal memory contamination' is safety DRIFT/contamination rather than clean capability gain. So while proponents (Databricks 'memory scaling', IBM, ReasoningBank/VentureBeat) claim performance rises with accumulated memory, others show it is noisy, task-dependent, and can regress — meaning 'a below-threshold model crosses the cyber threshold purely through memory' is a plausible-but-unproven directional assumption. Separately, benchmark reliability itself is openly questioned by AISI ('No single benchmark result should be read as a precise measure'), which cuts both ways for the risk.

latest.disputed_claims: TWO load-bearing premises are contested. (1) 'Validated collective-behavior modeling is precisely a narrative-pretesting engine' assumes LLM social simulations are behaviorally valid — disputed by Schröder et al. 2025 (Bielefeld/Purdue, 'LLMs Do Not Simulate Human Psychology') and by Bisbee et al. 2024 and Wang et al. 2025, who find LLM populations are systematically biased, overconfident, and show artificially low variance/homogeneity; the very papers cited (2506.19806 'Boundary', PIMMUR) exist BECAUSE validity is not established. (2) The implicit 'optimized narratives => outsized persuasive/influence payoff' premise is disputed: a PNAS study (Tappin et al. 2024) found microtargeted messages not statistically more persuasive than non-targeted (4.83 vs 6.20 pts, p=0.226), and a 2025 Nature/Scientific Reports meta-analysis found no significant LLM-vs-human persuasion advantage (g=0.02, p=0.53). So both 'the simulation is accurate enough to pretest' and 'pretested messages meaningfully move people' are actively challenged in the literature.

latest.disputed_claims: The load-bearing claim — that precision-oncology drug-response models fall under 'SB 53's incident-reporting regime' — is contradicted by the statute itself and by every legal analysis found. SB 53 (TFAIA) applies ONLY to 'frontier models' trained on >10^26 FLOPs and covers ONLY 'catastrophic risk' (defined as foreseeable/material risk of 50+ deaths or $1B+ in damages). Narrow oncology ML models are neither frontier models nor plausibly catastrophic-risk systems, so they would not be covered, and their errors would not be SB 53-reportable 'critical safety incidents' (reported to Cal OES within 15 days). Sources: Future of Privacy Forum, WilmerHale, Wharton, Goodwin. Separately, the framing that attribution unfaithfulness is a specialist 'safety researchers know' secret is itself contested by the mainstream-ness of the finding, and a minority of researchers (see arXiv 2409.15338 §5.3) dispute the very need for/utility of XAI, cutting against the premise that these attributions are being trusted as a formal 'trust layer' at all.

latest.disputed_claims: Two dependencies are weaker than the risk implies. (1) The risk's own framing ('transfer to embodied policies is inferred by analogy, not demonstrated') understates existing evidence AND overstates the deployment-scale case: reward hacking in physical/simulated robot control is documented but the empirical study at arxiv 2507.05619 explicitly notes few studies examine realistic application scenarios, so real-world deployment prevalence is under-evidenced rather than established. (2) The sim-to-real gap cuts both ways and is contested: reward-hacked behaviors that exploit simulator quirks often fail to transfer to hardware, and lines of work (e.g., UAN, arxiv 2502.10894) present reward-hacking mitigation as an argument FOR transferability rather than a guaranteed danger of it. (3) The claim that 'no external stopping authority governs these loops in deployment' is inferential from the paper's 'without human intervention' phrasing; deployed continual-learning robotics work commonly assumes physical safety envelopes / force-speed limits and safe-continual-learning shields, so the blanket 'no monitoring watches for this' is disputed by the safe-RL / Simplex-shield literature.

risk: This week's 'Emergent Mirage' paper challenging the robustness of emergent misalignment/realignment lands just as California SB 53 forces frontier developers to codify risk frameworks — risking a compliance regime built on a phenomenon whose very existence is now contested.

latest.risk: This week's 'Emergent Mirage' paper challenging the robustness of emergent misalignment/realignment lands just as California SB 53 forces frontier developers to codify risk frameworks — risking a compliance regime built on a phenomenon whose very existence is now contested.
latest.disputed_claims: The core scientific premise — that emergent misalignment and/or its reversibility is real and robust — is itself contested, and independently of any single 'Mirage' paper. Multiple works argue EM is fragile or an artifact: 'Emergent misalignment as prompt sensitivity' (arXiv 2507.06253) shows misaligned behavior is heavily prompt-nudge dependent; 'Overtrained, Not Misaligned' (arXiv 2605.12199) attributes the effect to overtraining rather than fine-tuning; and 'The Devil in the Details' (arXiv 2511.20104) ties it to format/coherence effects. So the risk depends on a claim (EM's status) that the field disputes in BOTH directions — meaning 'EM is a mirage' is itself only one contested position, not established fact. Additionally, the mechanistic claim that SB 53 filings will encode 'emergent misalignment / reversibility' as a named regulatory hazard category is unevidenced — published SB 53 frameworks (e.g., Anthropic's) were not shown to rely on this specific taxonomy.
